debug_points.description: The images show a pedestrian plaza or walkway area with stone/brick paver sidewalks and painted white curbs. The road surface visible appears to be smooth asphalt without any visible potholes or holes with missing material. A yellow pole (likely a street sign or barrier) is visible in the East-facing image.

The images show a road surface with significant standing water/flooding covering most of the visible area. While there is extensive water pooling, no actual potholes with visible missing asphalt, exposed base material, or broken edges can be confirmed. The water obscures the road surface but no holes breaking through the water surface are visible.

The Street View images show the camera positioned on or very close to the roof of a tour/rental vehicle. All images primarily show the vehicle's roof with various sponsor/company decals (360 Explora Panama, Hertz, Thrifty Car Rental, Tucán Country Club, Panama tourism logo). The road surface is not visible in any meaningful way to assess for potholes.

The images show a large area of standing water/flooding covering most of the visible road surface. The water appears murky brown/grey and completely obscures any view of the underlying pavement. While brick pavement is visible at the edges (north and northeast views), no actual potholes with missing material, exposed base layers, or broken edges can be confirmed beneath the floodwater.

The images show a paved road surface with a white vehicle parked nearby. The road appears wet with standing water visible in several frames, but no actual potholes with missing asphalt, exposed base material, or visible depth can be confirmed. The gray areas dominating most images appear to be standing water on intact pavement, not holes.

The images show a location that appears to be obscured by murky water or flooding. The majority of the frame across all angles shows a uniform olive-green/brown surface that appears to be standing water, not road surface. In the north and northeast views, vegetation (bamboo/palm-like plants) and a brick structure are visible at the edge, but no road surface or pavement damage is discernible.

These images show a paved area with concrete sidewalk tiles and asphalt road surface. There is significant water pooling/flooding covering most of the road surface, but no actual potholes with missing asphalt, exposed base material, or broken edges are visible. The water obscures the road surface but does not reveal any holes beneath it.

These images show a large body of murky water (appears to be a flooded area, pond, or water feature) with concrete/paved edges visible around the perimeter. The water surface is uniform gray-brown with no visible holes, broken edges, or exposed base material breaking the surface. This is not a road surface with potholes but rather a water body adjacent to paved areas.

The images show a flooded street with extensive standing water covering most of the road surface. While there is significant water pooling, I cannot see any actual holes, missing asphalt, exposed base material, or broken edges that would indicate a true pothole. The water obscures the road surface but there is no visible evidence of structural damage beneath it.

This appears to be a parking lot with marked parking spaces. The images show a sedan parked in a designated spot with yellow line markings. The pavement surface is grey asphalt/concrete that appears weathered but intact without any visible holes or missing material.

The images show a wet road surface with extensive standing water/puddles across multiple views. While there is significant water pooling visible in most frames, I cannot confirm any actual potholes - no visible holes with missing asphalt, exposed base material, broken edges, or discernible depth. The water appears to be pooling on intact but wet pavement.

Large standing water puddle covering significant portion of what appears to be an intersection or parking area. The water obscures the road surface, but there is no visible evidence of actual holes, broken edges, exposed base material, or missing asphalt. The visible pavement edges around the puddle show intact asphalt with yellow curb markings.

These images appear to be taken from an extremely low angle or possibly from within/near water, showing mostly grey/murky surfaces that appear to be water or flooded pavement. The visible road surfaces in some frames show asphalt with road markings but no visible potholes or holes with missing asphalt.

The Street View camera appears to be positioned at a very low angle, possibly mounted on a vehicle at street level. Most images show only partial views of parked vehicles and grey pavement surface. No potholes or holes with missing asphalt are visible in any of the 9 images.

These images show a body of water (appears to be a canal or waterway) with grassy banks and concrete/stone edging visible in some frames. The street view camera appears to be positioned over or very close to the water surface. No road surface is visible in any of the images, therefore no potholes can be identified.

The images show a parking area or road surface that appears wet with significant water pooling across multiple views. The gray area visible in most images is standing water on the pavement surface, not a pothole. No visible holes, missing asphalt, exposed base material, or broken edges can be identified.

The images show a large body of water (appears to be ocean or bay water) adjacent to a paved road surface. The camera appears to be positioned at a waterfront location in Panama City. No road damage or potholes are visible - the dark gray area dominating most frames is water, not damaged pavement.

This appears to be a large body of water (likely the Panama Canal based on coordinates near Panama City). The images show concrete dock/pier edge meeting water. No road surface or potholes are visible - this is waterfront infrastructure, not a roadway.

These images show a road surface that is largely obscured by what appears to be murky standing water or floodwater covering most of the visible area. The water is grey-brown and opaque, preventing clear visibility of the underlying road surface. In images 5 and 6, white road markings (directional arrows) are partially visible through shallow water on asphalt.

The images show a paved road surface with wet conditions and standing water. There are dark wet patches and some scattered leaves on the asphalt, but no actual holes with missing asphalt, exposed base material, or broken edges are visible. The dark areas are surface water pooling on intact pavement, not depressions with depth.
